source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

The cat sat on exactly 47 percent of the available chairs. Not the sofa. Not the floor. The chairs. Martin counted. He counted again. His wife said: stop counting. He didn't. Tuesday: three chairs. Wednesday: three chairs. Thursday: two. The deviation on Thursday troubled him deeply. He made a chart. The chart had eleven columns. His wife left on Friday.

Seven. That is how many times the door slammed before noon. Priya kept a tally on her palm. Her brother didn't notice the tally. He noticed the window instead — open, then closed, then open. The third slam was the loudest. (The fourth was actually louder, but memory edits.) Nobody apologized. The soup on the stove went cold. Priya ate it cold. Her brother left at 11:58. She checked. Two minutes before noon. The tally said seven. She washed her palm.

Five things were on the table: a key, a photograph, an envelope, her watch, and the watch had stopped at 3:17. She did not touch them. He had arranged them. (Or she had, and was lying.) The photograph faced down. Nobody mentioned this. Dinner was served at 6. She ate. He ate. The key remained. Later she said: I don't know whose it is. He said nothing. The watch still said 3:17. It always would.

The meeting ended at 2:43 — not 2:45, she checked her phone twice — and nobody said goodbye. Four people left first. (Or three. She wasn't counting.) The agenda had listed six items; they covered two. Item three was skipped without comment. Item four: 'Other business.' Nobody had other business. Outside: rain, then not rain, then rain. She took the long way home. Why? She didn't know. The meeting had been about nothing. She circled the block once. The notes she'd taken filled half a page. She crossed them all out.

She bought nine oranges. Not eight, not ten — nine. The receipt confirmed this. She checked the receipt at home, then checked again. The bag held eight. She counted the bag: eight. She counted again: eight. One orange was missing. (Or the receipt was wrong. Or she miscounted at the store.) She drove back. The store was closed. It was 9:04 PM. The store closed at 9. She sat in the car for six minutes. Then she went home. The eight oranges sat on the counter. She did not eat them that night. She did not eat them the next morning. On the third day, her husband asked: are those oranges? She said yes. He said: how many? She said nine.

The train was late. Not a little late. Forty-one minutes. Marcus stood on the platform and counted the pigeons: eleven. Then ten. (One flew.) He counted again: ten. He had a meeting at noon. It was 11:52. The train arrived at 11:53. He did not run. He walked. He sat down. Across from him: a woman with red shoes, a man with no bag, a child who didn't speak. The train reached his stop at 12:07. He was late. He knew this. He called no one. He walked into the meeting at 12:09. Someone had saved his seat. He didn't thank them. He sat. The meeting was already over.

She found the letter on Tuesday. (Or Wednesday. The postmark was smudged.) It had been opened. Not by her. The flap was torn, not cut. She placed it on the desk and left it there for three days. On the fourth day she read it. Twelve sentences. She counted. She read it again: twelve sentences. The last one said: don't come. She wasn't planning to. She wrote a reply. Four words. She crossed out two. Then crossed out the remaining two. She mailed the envelope anyway — empty. He would understand. (She did not know if he would understand.) The postage cost eighty-nine cents. She remembered this afterward. She did not remember the twelve sentences.

He counted the stairs: fourteen. He counted again: fourteen. (He had always thought thirteen. He had been wrong.) He climbed them. At the top: a door, half-open, and a light he hadn't left on. He stood on the landing for two minutes. Or four. He wasn't timing himself. Inside: a cup on the table, half-full of something cold. He touched the cup. He did not move the cup. He checked every room — three rooms, or four if you count the bathroom. He counted the bathroom. He found nothing. He went back to the stairs. He counted: fourteen. He sat on the seventh step. (Or the eighth. He didn't look down.) He waited. For what? He didn't know. At 11:17 he called his brother. His brother did not answer. He called again at 11:18. Nobody answered. He left no message. He sat on the step. The light was still on.

She called him at 7. No answer. She called at 7:03. No answer. She called seven more times. (She counted later: nine calls total.) She did not leave a message on any of them. At 8:15 she made tea. The kettle boiled. She did not pour the water. She sat at the table with the unlit stove and the full kettle and the phone face-down on the cloth. The cloth was blue. Or green. She couldn't say which now. At 9 she turned the phone over: no missed calls. At 9:01 he called. She did not pick up. (She tried to understand why she did not pick up. She could not explain it.) She let it ring six times, then seven. It stopped. She waited. He did not call again. She poured the tea. It was cold. She drank it anyway. She did not call back.

The jar had three coins in it. Or four. (She hadn't looked closely.) She put the jar on the shelf at 6:14 and did not touch it again until Saturday. Saturday came. She counted: four coins. One was foreign — she didn't know which country. She set it aside. She put the other three back. Then she took them out again. She lined them up: smallest to largest. Then largest to smallest. She put them back in the jar. She put the jar back on the shelf. At 6:14 again? No. It was 7:02. She noticed this. She did not know why she noticed. The foreign coin was still on the table. She did not pick it up. She turned off the light. The coin was still there in the dark. She knew this without looking. She went to bed. She did not sleep well. She had not slept well in eleven days. Or twelve.

She moved the chair three inches to the left. Then two inches back. (Or one inch. She didn't measure.) The room looked the same. She had expected it to look different. She sat in the chair. She stood up. She sat down again. This happened four times. She counted: four. She moved to the window. Outside: a car, parked. The same car as yesterday? She thought so. She wasn't sure. She wrote his name on a piece of paper. Then his phone number. Then she crossed out his name. The number remained. She stared at it for six minutes. (She checked the clock: six minutes exactly. She checked again: seven.) She did not dial the number. She folded the paper once. Then twice. Then tore it into — she counted — nine pieces. Or eight. The pieces were small. She put them in her pocket, not the bin. She didn't know why. The car outside had not moved. She checked twice.

She opened the envelope at 2:44. (She had not opened it at 2:43. She had held it.) Inside: two pages, or three. She counted. Three. The first page had seven lines. The second had four. The third had one line and a signature she did not recognize. She read the three pages. Then she read them again. Then a third time. (She was not counting the readings. She counted afterward: three.) She put the pages back in the envelope. She put the envelope on the counter. She walked to the sink. She did not wash her hands. She stood at the sink for — she checked the clock — eleven minutes. She walked back. The envelope was still there. She had known it would be. She picked it up. She put it down. She did not open it again. She said a word out loud. Just one word. She did not say it again. She could not have said which word.
